subject: Chemistry
title: Metallic Bonds: The Electron Sea
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Metallic Bonds: The Electron Sea.

hook.text: Metals conduct electricity because their outer electrons don't belong to any one atom—they flow freely like a sea.

buildup.text: Metal atoms release outer electrons into a shared pool. Positive ion cores sit in this 'electron sea.'

discovery.text: Free electrons carry charge (conductivity), transmit heat (thermal conductivity), and reflect light (metallic shine).

twist.text: Metals are malleable because ion layers can slide past each other without breaking bonds—the electron sea flows with them.

climax.text: Alloys mix different metal atoms, disrupting the regular lattice. This makes them harder—steel is stronger than pure iron.

punchline.text: Electrons free to roam. That sea of charge is why metals conduct, shine, and bend.

quiz.question: Why are metals malleable (able to be hammered into shape)?
options: Ion layers slide past each other while the electron sea maintains the bond
Metal atoms are very small and can move easily
Heat from hammering melts the metal slightly
